research and question (no code edits):
in the outline-decorations experiments (see @docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md and related docs), we have researched and discarded the approach with per-block editor tree approach. but then in experiment 5a we have discovered that
> Obsidian renders an actively-edited table cell in Live Preview as its own separate, independent CM6 EditorView, mounted inside the outer table widget’s own DOM

aren't these two things related? I don't quite understand the internals and maybe these are completely different things, but it sounds to me that the way Obsidian provides tables WYSIWIG live-preview editing experience is by "drawing" the actual table cells and making each of them an independent editor view. why can't we do the same thing with the outline nodes tree? have a structural tree that looks like a tree and behaves like one, and only when user clicks into one of the nodes, they interact with the embedded editor view for that node's content. 

please explain it in simple terms. 

also, I think one of the assumptions about the researched obsidian-continuous-journal plugin (that I've experimented with in parallel) was that it would require usage of some private APIs and it disqualifies the whole approach. but I actually submitted a draft of the plugin to the registry to run a scan and it hasn't flagged any major issues, so maybe the assumption about that approach wasn't correct and could be revisited (if relevant)

research and question (no code edits):
in the outline-decorations experiments (see @docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md and related docs), we have researched and discarded the approach with per-block editor tree approach. but then in experiment 5a we have discovered that
> Obsidian renders an actively-edited table cell in Live Preview as its own separate, independent CM6 EditorView, mounted inside the outer table widget’s own DOM

aren't these two things related? I don't quite understand the internals and maybe these are completely different things, but it sounds to me that the way Obsidian provides tables WYSIWIG live-preview editing experience is by "drawing" the actual table cells and making each of them an independent editor view. why can't we do the same thing with the outline nodes tree? have a structural tree that looks like a tree and behaves like one, and only when user clicks into one of the nodes, they interact with the embedded editor view for that node's content. 

please explain it in simple terms. 

also, I think one of the assumptions about the researched obsidian-continuous-journal plugin (that I've experimented with in parallel) was that it would require usage of some private APIs and it disqualifies the whole approach. but I actually submitted a draft of the plugin to the registry to run a scan and it hasn't flagged any major issues, so maybe the assumption about that approach wasn't correct and could be revisited (if relevant)

# Decoration experiments: plan & results (2026-07-13 → 2026-07-19)

Answers the postmortem's open question — is cross-kind visual unification achievable, and
how — by running small, isolated, falsifiable prototypes instead of another multi-attempt
scramble. See [06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md](06-outline-decorations-postmortem.md)
for what failed and why, and the git history of this doc's own introduction for the
external research that shaped this plan (obsidian-outliner's measure-don't-fight technique,
Logseq's per-block-component architecture, Silverbullet's declined-to-unify precedent).

**All experiments have concluded.** This document is the series hub: the shared ground
rules and fixture corpus, the final results table, and the two experiments that were never
triggered. Each completed experiment's full design and results (including every bug's
history) lives in its own document; the cross-experiment lessons are accumulated
separately. Earlier revisions of this file also carried the per-experiment setup mechanics
and self-contained handoff prompts used to launch each experiment session — those served
their purpose and are preserved in this file's git history, not here.

| Doc | Contents |
| --- | --- |
| [08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md](08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md) | Experiment 1: additive-only indentation — design, results, 3 real bugs. **Keep** — the foundation everything else builds on |
| [09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md](09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md) | Experiment 2: guide lines, 2a (pixel-measured overlay) vs. 2b (CSS stacked-gradient) head to head — **2b chosen** |
| [10-experiment-5-block-markers.md](10-experiment-5-block-markers.md) | Experiment 5: per-kind block markers, 5a (SVG icons, DOM mechanism) vs. 5b (CSS shapes → uniform dot) head to head — **5a chosen**; includes the comparison verdict and the hardening checklist for productionizing 5a |
| [11-decoration-lessons.md](11-decoration-lessons.md) | Accumulated cross-experiment findings, grouped by theme — read before touching decorations, CM6 extensions, or native-chrome interactions |

## Results

Final status of every experiment in the series. The chosen implementation path is
**1 → 2b → 5a** (PR stack: #8 → #10 → #11/#12); 2a and 5b were fully built, verified,
and closed in favor of their head-to-head counterparts, with all learnings preserved.

| # | Technique | Status | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Additive indentation, no marker | Done, three real bugs found and fixed — [details](08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md) | **Keep** — merging (PR #8) |
| 2a | Guides — overlay-measured | Done, three real bugs found and fixed — [details](09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md) | Fully capable, **superseded by 2b** (simpler at equal coverage); PR #9 to be closed |
| 2b | Guides — CSS stacked-gradient | Done, full corpus coverage confirmed — [details](09-experiment-2-guide-lines.md) | **Keep — chosen** (PR #10): full parity with 2a at smaller code size, zero pixel measurement |
| 3 | Minimal marker fallback (conditional) | Not triggered — see below | Deprioritized; the marker question was later answered properly by Experiment 5 |
| 4 | Widget-spacer spike (optional) | Not triggered — see below | No fragility observed that would call for it |
| 5a | Block markers — real icons, DOM widget mechanism | Done; four follow-up rounds (fold-chevron fix, centered placement, visibility setting, nested-editor leak found & fixed) — [details](10-experiment-5-block-markers.md) | **Keep — chosen** (PR #12, merged): mechanism solves vertical alignment/opacity/coupling by construction; icons proved expressive on real content |
| 5b | Block markers — CSS shapes → uniform dot | Done; pivoted from 8 per-kind shapes to one dot after real-content review; 8 real bugs found and fixed — [details](10-experiment-5-block-markers.md) | Closed (PR #13): mechanism superseded by 5a (shared-opacity limit, coordinate coupling, live-measurement irony); design restraint lesson and findings preserved in [10](10-experiment-5-block-markers.md)/[11](11-decoration-lessons.md) |

## Ground rules for every experiment below

1. **One technique per experiment, isolated.** Each gets its own branch off a clean
   baseline (outline mode + grammar + structural ops, zero decoration code) so results are
   attributable and comparable, not entangled the way attempt 1→2→3 was last time.
2. **A fixed, shared fixture corpus, screenshotted every time — not just the fixture for
   the thing being fixed.** Every experiment runs against the *same* corpus (below), in
   both bundled light and dark themes. Growing the corpus is fine; shrinking scope of what
   gets screenshotted is not.
3. **A real-vault pass is mandatory before an experiment is called done**, not just the
   synthetic corpus. This was the single biggest false-confidence source last time.
4. **Record results in the comparison table (the Results section above) before moving to
   the next experiment.** Verdict is one of: keep, reject, or needs-follow-up — with the concrete
   reason.
5. **No experiment is "done" on green tests alone.** Unit tests here can only prove the
   pure computation is internally consistent; they cannot prove anything about rendering.
   Screenshot + real-vault verification is the actual gate.

## The fixture corpus (shared across all experiments)

- **Flat**: 3 top-level paragraphs, no lists or headings at all — the original motivating
  bug (`04-open-questions.md`: "with no bullets/indent chrome, outline mode is hard to
  verify by eye in flat documents").
- **Mixed**: heading → heading → nested bullet list → paragraph-as-sibling-of-list-item →
  code fence, matching `MIXED_MD` in the existing e2e spec.
- **Checkbox/task**: a top-level paragraph followed by a checkbox list, mixed
  checked/unchecked — the exact shape that broke in the field last time.
- **Heading-then-list, no paragraph**: a list directly under a heading with *no*
  intervening paragraph — isolates the "list item's native indentation doesn't know about
  heading ancestors" case, the crux of the additive-margin hypothesis.
- **Multi-line continuation**: a paragraph and a list item each spanning 2+ lines via
  `Shift+Enter` — never actually screenshotted last time; explicitly in scope now.
- **Wide numbering**: an ordered list crossing a digit-width boundary (items 9 and 10) —
  checks whether any supplemental indentation clashes with native marker-width variance.
- **Deep nesting**: 4+ levels mixing bullet and ordered markers.

Committed once as [e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts](../../e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts) —
exported markdown-string constants (matching the project's existing `createNote(path,
content)` convention, not on-disk `.md` files) plus an `ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES` array for
"screenshot everything" loops. Every experiment branch imports from here; don't fork or
re-type fixtures per experiment, or results stop being comparable.

---

> **Corpus growth since this section was written** (ground rule #2 allows growing, never
> shrinking): Experiment 1's real-vault bugs added **widget-atoms**; Experiment 2b's table
> finding added **wide-table**; Experiment 5's shared prerequisite promoted a **quote**
> fixture. [e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts](../../e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts) is the
> authoritative list.

## Experiment 3 (conditional — only run if Experiment 1's flat fixture fails review)

**Trigger**: if dropping the marker means the **flat** fixture (3 top-level paragraphs, all
at depth 0, so indentation alone conveys nothing) still doesn't read as "outline mode is
on" — which is the *original* motivating bug this whole change exists to fix — try a
minimal, low-risk marker that avoids the em/rem bug class *by construction*: a fixed-px-
width left border or background tint (not a `::before` glyph with a font-size), so there's
no font-size context to inherit incorrectly regardless of which kind's line it's on.

**Success criteria**: flat fixture reads as distinct nodes at a glance, in a screenshot,
without reintroducing any font-size-dependent sizing.

**If this also isn't good enough**: fall back to the overlay-measured marker (same
mechanism as 2a, sized as a fixed absolute value, never inheriting font-size by
construction) — this is the "real" fix for the original marker-size bug, just held in
reserve since it's more implementation work than the compromise Experiment 1 proposes.

**Outcome.** Not triggered in practice. Its trigger condition (flat fixture unreadable) technically
fires, but our real-content read is that it's not actually a legibility gap (see
Experiment 1's "also confirmed not a bug" note in
[08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md](08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md)). Markers are judged less important than
initially scoped now that indentation alone reads as hierarchy.

## Experiment 4 (optional risk-spike — only if Experiment 1 still feels fragile on real content)

Spike the `Decoration.widget` spacer technique (real inline content pushing text via layout
width, instead of any CSS box-model property) in isolation, specifically against the
multi-line/wrapped-continuation fixture, to test the one confirmed real gotcha found in
research: CM6 wraps every widget in an invisible `.cm-widgetBuffer` that can introduce
stray line-wrap points. Go in expecting this might kill the idea — it's unproven in the
wild for exactly this reason, and the whole CM6 community converged on CSS `Decoration.line`
instead. Only worth spending time on if Experiment 1's `padding-left`/`margin-left`
approach keeps showing cascade fragility against real themes despite the additive-only
discipline.

**Outcome.** Not triggered. Experiment 1 showed no cascade fragility against the synthetic corpus or
real vault notes once its three bugs were fixed
(see [08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md](08-experiment-1-additive-indentation.md)) — nothing suggests the
`padding-left`/`margin-left` approach is running out of road.

## Verification infrastructure (Track 5 — applied across all experiments)

- Commit the fixture corpus once; every experiment's verification re-screenshots all of it,
  not just its own new fixture.
- One automated check: render two elements of deliberately different font-size (a heading
  line and a paragraph line) at the same computed tree depth, diff *every*
  decoration-related computed style at once (`padding-left`, `margin-left`, any marker
  `font-size`/`width`/`height`). This single mechanism would have caught both the
  indentation em/rem bug and the marker-size em/rem bug last time, instead of two separate
  manual catches.
- Fix `npm run build` to actually invoke esbuild, or add an explicit pre-verification step
  (`npm run build:plugin && npm run vault:install`) to the checklist so a stale bundle
  can't produce a false "verified" result again.
- A real dev-vault pass is part of *every* experiment's exit criteria, not a final step
  after all experiments conclude.

## Architecture question: per-block editor tree — resolved, not prototyped

Researched via the author's `continuous-journal` plugin as the concrete test case.
Verdict: not a lighter alternative to the already-rejected custom-view path — it requires a
comparable-or-larger private-API footprint (leaf-grafting, `setActiveLeaf` monkey-patching)
plus a genuinely unsolved new problem (N editors bound to overlapping ranges of one shared
file, kept consistent as any one of them edits). Shelved against this project's decided
100%-public-API bar (Q1); revisit only if that bar itself is ever renegotiated.

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- openspec-apply-change: Implement tasks from an OpenSpec change. Use when the user wants to start implementing, continue implementation, or work through tasks.
- openspec-archive-change: Archive a completed change in the experimental workflow. Use when the user wants to finalize and archive a change after implementation is complete.
- openspec-explore: Enter explore mode - a thinking partner for exploring ideas, investigating problems, and clarifying requirements. Use when the user wants to think through something before or during a change.
- openspec-propose: Propose a new change with all artifacts generated in one step. Use when the user wants to quickly describe what they want to build and get a complete proposal with design, specs, and tasks ready for implementation.
- openspec-sync-specs: Sync delta specs from a change to main specs. Use when the user wants to update main specs with changes from a delta spec, without archiving the change.
- openspec-update-change: Update an OpenSpec change by revising its existing planning artifacts and keeping them coherent with one another. Use when the user wants to revise a change's plan, fold new decisions into it, or reconcile its artifacts after an edit. Never edits code.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:doc-coauthoring: Guide users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation. Use when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content. This workflow helps users efficiently transfer context, refine content through iteration, and verify the doc works for readers. Trigger when user mentions writing docs, creating proposals, drafting specs, or similar documentation tasks.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mct-cbt-therapy: AI-assisted therapy integrating Metacognitive Therapy (MCT) and Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT). Use when conducting therapeutic conversations, providing mental health support, or when user seeks help with anxiety, depression, worry, rumination, negative thought patterns, or emotional distress. Triggers on therapy-related requests, mental health discussions, or explicit requests for MCT/CBT techniques.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use when: morning brief, daily brief, set up my morning brief, what's my day look like, run my brief.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:phenomenology-guide: Comparative contemplative philosophy guide for understanding experiences in meditation and daily life. Use when user asks "what is this state?", "why does X happen in practice?", "what did tradition Y believe about Z?", wants to understand contemplative phenomena, seeks connections between traditions, or wants to learn history of philosophy, theology, and traditional metaphysics alongside naturalized interpretations. Maps experiences to both scientific mechanisms and traditional frameworks without forcing metaphysical commitment.

This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. Use when user wants to build a practice routine, asks "what should I practice?", needs help sequencing practices, feels scattered across techniques, wants to develop consistency, struggles with habit discipline, or asks about integrating different traditions. Provides structure while respecting individual constraints and preferences. Emphasizes minimal viable practice over ambitious programs that collapse.

- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.

- anthropic-skills:somatic-navigator: Body-based nervous system regulation and interoceptive awareness guide. - anthropic-skills:values-clarity: Socratic dialogue for exploring what matters, clarifying personal values, and addressing values-action gaps. Use when user questions life direction, feels meaningless or stuck, experiences conflict between what they do and what matters to them, wants to understand their priorities, or asks about purpose, meaning, or "what should I do with my life." Integrates ACT values work, Stoic virtue ethics, and naturalized Buddhist ethics without metaphysical baggage.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. - dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## deepwiki
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_automation_manage: Manage Devin automations — list, get, create, update, delete, or fetch the trigger event schemas (private mode only)
- devin_code_scan_manage: Manage Devin code scans — list scans, list findings, list profiles, get a profile (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_session_create: Create one or more child Devin sessions (private mode only)
- devin_session_interact: Manage a Devin session — get status, send messages, sleep/terminate/archive, read messages & attachments, manage tags (private mode only)
- devin_session_events: Inspect session events — list summaries, fetch full details, or search event contents (private mode only)
- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)
- list_integrations: List all native integrations and MCP servers with their status and settings URLs (private mode only)
- find_setting: Find Devin webapp settings pages and deep-link URLs for them (private mode only)

content.content:
280	having the setting's own setter toggle outline mode off then immediately back
281	on (via the registry directly, not the user-facing command) — guaranteeing two
282	genuinely different decoration outputs (none vs. the real thing) that CM6
283	always detects, reliably re-triggering `docViewUpdate` twice. This also
284	retroactively fixed the identical latent gap in the earlier placement setting
285	(round 2), which no test happened to exercise against a table-only note.
286	
287	**Follow-up round 4: a genuine architectural bug, found via a flaky test —
288	decorations leaking into Obsidian's own nested per-cell editors.** While
289	verifying the visibility setting's e2e coverage, one specific test (visibility
290	`'headings-and-paragraphs'` against a table) failed intermittently — first
291	misdiagnosed as an async timing race in the table widget's own DOM settling (a
292	`browser.pause()` was gambling against however long that took) and "fixed"
293	with a poll-based wait instead of a fixed sleep, which is a legitimate,
294	permanent improvement in its own right (`waitForContentChildCount` in
295	`e2e/helpers.ts`, mirroring the existing `waitForNotice` pattern) but did *not*
296	fix the underlying test — it kept failing deterministically once actual
297	machine load (which had been intermittently masking/unmasking it across runs)
298	settled down enough to reproduce reliably.
299	
300	Root-caused by tracing the stray marker element's full DOM ancestry: Obsidian
301	renders an actively-edited table cell (cursor inside it) as its own separate,
302	independent CM6 `EditorView`, mounted inside the outer table widget's own DOM
303	(`.cm-embed-block.cm-table-widget` → `.table-wrapper` → `<table>` → `<tr>` →
304	`<th>`/`<td>` → `.table-cell-wrapper` → a whole nested `.cm-editor`). This
305	plugin's `registerEditorExtension` (`main.ts`) applies its decorations to
306	*every* CM6 instance Obsidian creates app-wide — this nested one included — and
307	that nested editor's own "document" is just the cell's raw text (e.g. a single
308	word), which `decorate()`/`parse()` classifies as a plain paragraph (the
309	default block kind for a bare line with no special syntax) — not an atom, so
310	under `'all'`/`'headings-and-paragraphs'` visibility it becomes marker-eligible
311	and picks up both a stray marker icon *and* depth-based padding/margin exactly
312	like a real top-level line, visibly corrupting the cell being edited. This is a
313	real, live, user-facing bug, not just a test artifact — confirmed independently
314	by the author noticing it in their own use before this was reported back.
315	
316	The state-only "is this note in outline mode" gate (`editorInfoField`, already
317	used throughout `decorations.ts` as a reliable gate everywhere else) cannot
318	distinguish the nested editor from the real top-level one — confirmed live via
319	`EditorView.findFromDOM()` on the nested instance that its own `editorInfoField`
320	resolves to the exact *same* outer `MarkdownView` object as the real note. Only
321	DOM ancestry can tell them apart: a real top-level note's own `.cm-editor` is
322	never itself nested inside a `.cm-embed-block` (those are its own descendants,
323	never its ancestors). Since `StateField.create`/`update` have no view/DOM

file.content:
having the setting's own setter toggle outline mode off then immediately back
on (via the registry directly, not the user-facing command) — guaranteeing two
genuinely different decoration outputs (none vs. the real thing) that CM6
always detects, reliably re-triggering `docViewUpdate` twice. This also
retroactively fixed the identical latent gap in the earlier placement setting
(round 2), which no test happened to exercise against a table-only note.

**Follow-up round 4: a genuine architectural bug, found via a flaky test —
decorations leaking into Obsidian's own nested per-cell editors.** While
verifying the visibility setting's e2e coverage, one specific test (visibility
`'headings-and-paragraphs'` against a table) failed intermittently — first
misdiagnosed as an async timing race in the table widget's own DOM settling (a
`browser.pause()` was gambling against however long that took) and "fixed"
with a poll-based wait instead of a fixed sleep, which is a legitimate,
permanent improvement in its own right (`waitForContentChildCount` in
`e2e/helpers.ts`, mirroring the existing `waitForNotice` pattern) but did *not*
fix the underlying test — it kept failing deterministically once actual
machine load (which had been intermittently masking/unmasking it across runs)
settled down enough to reproduce reliably.

Root-caused by tracing the stray marker element's full DOM ancestry: Obsidian
renders an actively-edited table cell (cursor inside it) as its own separate,
independent CM6 `EditorView`, mounted inside the outer table widget's own DOM
(`.cm-embed-block.cm-table-widget` → `.table-wrapper` → `<table>` → `<tr>` →
`<th>`/`<td>` → `.table-cell-wrapper` → a whole nested `.cm-editor`). This
plugin's `registerEditorExtension` (`main.ts`) applies its decorations to
*every* CM6 instance Obsidian creates app-wide — this nested one included — and
that nested editor's own "document" is just the cell's raw text (e.g. a single
word), which `decorate()`/`parse()` classifies as a plain paragraph (the
default block kind for a bare line with no special syntax) — not an atom, so
under `'all'`/`'headings-and-paragraphs'` visibility it becomes marker-eligible
and picks up both a stray marker icon *and* depth-based padding/margin exactly
like a real top-level line, visibly corrupting the cell being edited. This is a
real, live, user-facing bug, not just a test artifact — confirmed independently
by the author noticing it in their own use before this was reported back.

The state-only "is this note in outline mode" gate (`editorInfoField`, already
used throughout `decorations.ts` as a reliable gate everywhere else) cannot
distinguish the nested editor from the real top-level one — confirmed live via
`EditorView.findFromDOM()` on the nested instance that its own `editorInfoField`
resolves to the exact *same* outer `MarkdownView` object as the real note. Only
DOM ancestry can tell them apart: a real top-level note's own `.cm-editor` is
never itself nested inside a `.cm-embed-block` (those are its own descendants,
never its ancestors). Since `StateField.create`/`update` have no view/DOM
access at all, fixing this required moving both decoration `StateField`s
(`computeDecorations`/`computeMarkers`) to `ViewPlugin`s (which do have
`view.dom`), gating each — and `MarginCompensation`, already a ViewPlugin — on
a shared `isNestedEditor(view)` check (`view.dom.closest('.cm-embed-block') !==
null`). Confirmed fixed via the same DOM-ancestry trace, and via two
consecutive clean e2e runs of the affected test (previously flaky, now
deterministic) plus the full 8-spec-file suite (58 tests) twice with no
regressions.

**Code cost of rounds 2-4 combined**: ~20 lines in `decorate.ts`
(`hasChildren`), ~15 lines in `mode-registry.ts` (`MarkerVisibility` type +
setting), ~15 lines in `main.ts` (setter + the off/on `forceRedraw` toggle +
settings dropdown), net ~+85 lines in `decorations.ts` after removing the
placement-variant machinery (round 2) and adding the nested-editor guard (round
4: two new small `ViewPlugin` wrapper classes replacing two `StateField`s, plus
`isNestedEditor`/`shouldShowMarker`).

## Results — Experiment 5b: CSS-shape markers (background-layer mechanism)

**Verdict: keep**, pending the head-to-head against 5a. Branched off
`experiment/decorations-2b-guides-css-gradient`. All 9 corpus fixtures (the shared 8, plus
a `quote`-kind fixture promoted into `ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES` per this experiment's own
shared prerequisite) screenshotted, both bundled themes, plus targeted computed-style
assertions on the resolved `::after` background-image — see
`e2e/specs/52-block-markers-shapes.e2e.ts` (on the closed 5b branch
`claude/decorations-block-markers-595e13` — 5b's code never merged),

content.content:
90	- **Grammar**: high-precedence keymaps for Enter/Shift+Enter/Tab/moves (proven).
91	- **Node UX**: decorations for bullets/handles/fold chevrons; block-select highlight via line
92	  decorations; zoom via replace-decorations + breadcrumbs (proven by obsidian-zoom).
93	- **Scope control**: extensions are registered globally but no-op unless the file is an outline
94	  document (frontmatter flag / folder / global mode — open question), checked via metadataCache.
95	
96	*Pros*: 100% public API; perfect-scorecard compatible; mobile works; degrades gracefully (files
97	are plain markdown lists everywhere else); interops with the whole ecosystem (backlinks, graph,
98	search, sync, other plugins). *Cons*: we inherit markdown-view constraints — the DOM is CM6's
99	line-based DOM, so some pixel-perfect block UI (e.g., Notion-style hover menus per block) takes
100	more creativity; "hard" enforcement is a filter over a text buffer, which demands a rigorous,
101	well-tested invariant layer (this is exactly the pure tree-ops + property-test discipline from
102	the TUI prototype).
103	
104	### Option B — Custom view: parse md → own block editor (Kanban/Excalidraw pattern)
105	
106	A `TextFileView` renders the outline as a fully custom (e.g., per-node contenteditable/CM)
107	block UI; the file stays markdown on disk.
108	
109	The blocker: **there is no public mechanism to open `.md` files in a custom view.**
110	Kanban does it by monkey-patching `WorkspaceLeaf.setViewState` (confirmed in source: intercepts
111	`state.type === 'markdown'`, checks for its frontmatter key, rewrites to its view type) — a
112	private-API hack, fragile, and contrary to this project's bar. A
113	[2025 forum thread](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/custom-viewer-ui-for-certain-markdown-files/108104)
114	asking for an official alternative got no answer from the team. Alternatives all lose something
115	essential: a custom extension (`.outline`) forfeits metadataCache/links/graph/mobile-editor
116	interop; "open via command" per file is UX friction and re-breaks on every workspace restore.
117	
118	*Nuance discovered later (2026-07-13, from the
119	[workflowy-style-outline](https://github.com/springrain1/workflowy-style-outline) case study —
120	see the plugin-landscape doc)*: the monkey-patch is only needed for **automatic** association.
121	An **explicit, user-invoked toggle** — a command/menu item calling
122	`leaf.setViewState({type: OUR_VIEW})` on the active leaf — is plain public API, and workspace
123	persistence keeps a toggled leaf in that view across restarts. So a scorecard-clean custom view
124	is *reachable*; what remains against Option B is everything else that project demonstrates:
125	rebuilding the editor (per-block textareas, IME/mobile/undo/save-queue re-implementations,
126	an "isolation layer" to avoid breaking native Obsidian) and, in their case, a lossy
127	md↔outline conversion — the anti-example for our isomorphism requirement.
128	
129	*Verdict*: keep as a possible far-future layer (the explicit-toggle variant lowers the API
130	barrier but not the engineering cost); not a foundation compatible with today's goals.
131	
132	### Option C — Hybrid (A + owned side surfaces)
133	
134	Option A for editing, plus our own `registerView` panes where we fully control rendering:
135	**structured backlinks pane** (tree context from `listItems`, editable via `Vault.process` or
136	embedded editors), zoom breadcrumb header, optional outline-wide search/filter view. All public.
137	This captures most of what Option B wanted anyway, without the takeover problem.
138	
139	## Feasibility scorecard per target feature
140	
141	| Target behavior | Public-API path | Confidence |
142	| --- | --- | --- |
143	| Enter/Tab/move/merge grammar | CM6 keymaps (proven in prod) | High |
144	| Structure enforcement | `transactionFilter` invariant layer | High (novel engineering, proven primitives) |
145	| Node selection model | transactionFilter + decorations (pro-outliner v1 exists) | High |
146	| Fold + persistence | native folds; storage decision open (plugin data vs in-file) | High |
147	| Zoom + breadcrumbs | replace-decorations (proven: obsidian-zoom) | High |
148	| Drag & drop | CM6 drop + widgets (proven: obsidian-outliner) | High |
149	| Multiline nodes | md continuation lines + grammar keymaps | Medium-high (careful spec needed) |
150	| Block refs/embeds | native `^id` / `![[#^id]]` | High (core does the heavy lifting) |
151	| Mirrors (synced blocks) | native embeds first; true editable mirrors later | Medium |
152	| Structured backlinks pane (read) | own view + `resolvedLinks` + `listItems` | High |
153	| Editable backlinks | + `Vault.process`/embedded editor per block | Medium |
154	| Mobile | all of the above is CM6/DOM | High (test discipline) |
155	| Perfect scorecard | eslint-plugin from day 1; no `any`-casts into internals | High under Option A |
156	
157	Two honesty notes: (a) `getBacklinksForFile` is **not** public — the public path is
158	`resolvedLinks` + own reverse index (the [backlink-cache](https://github.com/mnaoumov/obsidian-backlink-cache)
159	plugin shows the pattern); (b) even obsidian-outliner reaches for `(editor as any).cm` — we can
160	avoid it almost everywhere by living inside registered extensions (a `ViewPlugin` receives its
161	`EditorView` directly), which should be an explicit engineering rule.
162	
163	## Addendum: the universal isomorphic outline view (post-alignment)
164	
165	The 2026-07-12 alignment (see [04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md), Q2) widened the
166	document model from "lists" to the **full markdown block tree** — headings, paragraphs, code
167	fences, quotes, tables *and* list items are all nodes; the outline is a toggleable editor UI
168	over any note, with a lossless (isomorphic) round-trip. Feasibility notes for that vision:
169

The user's second point about the registry scan missing private APIs makes sense too, and I can see the docs already acknowledge this: the automated review scan explicitly doesn't check for monkey-patching or private API usage. So the scan passing doesn't contradict the assumption—it's just operating within the project's own defined scope, and the fragility concern still stands.

Now I'm clarifying the relationship between Obsidian's nested table-cell editor and the per-block editor idea. They're related but have crucial differences: Obsidian's implementation runs from inside the app with full access to internals and sync control, whereas plugins lack that machinery. The nested cell editor only exists for one cell at a time while the cursor is active, then gets destroyed and replaced with static DOM—so the sync problem is much smaller. Tables are treated as atomic blocks by the outer editor, meaning there's no double-ownership of the same text being rendered both as editable and static. An outline would need to replace essentially the entire document view, which creates a fundamentally different problem.

The core issue is that Obsidian doesn't expose hooks for "this is a nested editor"—it's all internal machinery. A plugin could theoretically create its own EditorViews and embed them via widget decorations, but that runs into the overlapping ranges problem: outline nodes nest, so a parent node's range contains its children's ranges. You'd either need to flatten it to leaf-only editors with tree chrome around them, or accept the overlapping ranges that the architecture doesn't cleanly support.

Even if you built raw CM6 editors, you'd lose all of Obsidian's markdown rendering, link autocomplete, and plugin extensions inside each node—you'd get a bare editor, not an Obsidian editor. The Lapel confirmation drives this home: even Obsidian team members acknowledge there's no official API to scope extensions away from these nested editors, which shows how non-public this whole system is.

The continuous-journal scan wouldn't catch monkey-patching or private-API use anyway—the docs already flagged that the automated check doesn't look for those. The real bar was always self-imposed: fragility across Obsidian updates, mobile compatibility, and ecosystem interop concerns.

If your own bar is the actual gate, you can renegotiate that—the docs explicitly say to revisit only if that bar itself changes. content.text:
Good question — the two things are genuinely related, and you've spotted the right connection. Let me explain what Obsidian is actually doing with table cells, why that trick doesn't transfer to a whole outline the way it might seem, and then address the continuous-journal point (where the docs actually predicted your scan result).

## Yes, the table cell is a mini per-block editor — done by Obsidian, from the inside

What we found in Experiment 5a ([10-experiment-5-block-markers.md:300](docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md:300)) is exactly the pattern you describe: the table is a "drawn" structural widget, and the one cell you're editing gets a real, live editor mounted inside it. So conceptually, yes — Obsidian's table editing *is* a per-block editor tree, in miniature. The reason we can't lift that pattern for outline nodes comes down to who is doing it and at what scale:

**1. Obsidian does it with its own internals, which are not offered to plugins.** That nested cell editor isn't a bare CodeMirror instance — it's a full Obsidian markdown editor (Live Preview rendering, link autocomplete, the app's whole extension stack) that Obsidian constructs using its private editor-factory machinery, and Obsidian's own private code keeps the cell's tiny document synced with the real file underneath. A plugin can create raw CM6 `EditorView`s (that part is public), but what you get is a plain text box — not an Obsidian editor. To make each outline node feel like Obsidian, you'd have to either reach into the private factory or rebuild Live Preview yourself. That's the same wall as the custom-view Option B in [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:104](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:104).

**2. Obsidian only ever does it for *one* cell at a time.** This is the part that makes their version tractable and ours not. The nested editor exists only while your cursor is in that cell; click away and it's torn down and replaced by static rendered HTML. So at any moment there is exactly one small live editor, over one small, self-contained slice of text. The "N editors bound to overlapping ranges of one shared file, kept consistent as any one of them edits" problem — the unsolved problem named in [07-decoration-experiments-plan.md:147](docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md:147) — simply never arises for them, because N=1 by design. An outline view wants the whole tree visible and clickable at once, and outline nodes *nest* (a parent's text range contains its children's), which is strictly harder than a grid of disjoint cells.

**3. The table is an atomic island; the outline is the whole document.** The outer editor treats the entire table as one opaque replacement widget — a single "atom" it doesn't try to render as text. That's why there's no double-ownership fight: the outer view shows the widget, the inner view owns the text. To do this for an outline you'd have to widget-replace essentially *every line of the document*, leaving the outer CM6 view as an empty shell whose only job is hosting widgets. At that point you haven't extended Obsidian's editor — you've replaced it, which is precisely the custom-view path the project already rejected on engineering-cost grounds (IME, mobile, undo, save queues — the workflowy-style-outline anti-example in [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:118](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:118)).

**4. Our own bug report is evidence of how non-public this machinery is.** The nested cell editor is invisible to every public API: its `editorInfoField` resolves to the *same* `MarkdownView` as the real editor, and only DOM-ancestry sniffing (`closest('.cm-embed-block')`) can tell them apart. Even the Lapel plugin's Obsidian-team reviewer said there's "no official way to not load an editor extension inside table cells" ([10-experiment-5-block-markers.md:848](docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md:848)). If Obsidian doesn't even expose "you are inside a nested editor" as a signal, it certainly doesn't expose "here's how to make your own."

So the simple summary: **Obsidian's table trick proves the architecture works when the host app does it — one editor at a time, over an atomic island, with private glue. It doesn't prove a plugin can do it, at document scale, over nested ranges, with public glue.** The discovery and the rejection are consistent, not contradictory.

## The continuous-journal scan result: expected, and it doesn't overturn the reasoning — but it does sharpen one question

Your clean registry scan actually confirms something the research already recorded rather than contradicting it: the automated review explicitly does **not** check for monkey-patching or private-API access. That's stated in both [02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md:141](docs/research/02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md:141) ("Notably absent: any check for monkey-patching or private-API access — those surface in human review") and [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:26](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:26). So "the scanner didn't flag it" was the predicted outcome even for a plugin that leans on private APIs — it's evidence about the scanner, not about the plugin.

The per-block approach was shelved on three grounds, and the scan only touches the weakest one:

1. **Registry acceptance** — your experiment shows the automated gate is passable; human review might still push back on monkey-patching (it's visible in source), but this barrier is evidently lower than a hard "disqualified."
2. **The project's own self-imposed bar** — "100% public API, no hacks" was a *decision* (Q1), made for fragility reasons: private surfaces break silently across Obsidian updates, especially on mobile. The doc explicitly leaves the door open: "revisit only if that bar itself is ever renegotiated" ([07-decoration-experiments-plan.md:154](docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md:154)). Your scan result is a legitimate input to renegotiating it — but it's a values question (how much update-fragility you'll accept), not a facts question the scan settles.
3. **The unsolved engineering problem** — N live editors over nested ranges of one file, kept consistent. This one is untouched by the scan, and it's the load-bearing objection. Even with the API bar dropped to zero, nobody in the ecosystem has demonstrated this working (continuous-journal included — its trick is grafting whole *separate files'* editors into one pane, which is a much easier problem than splitting *one* file across many editors).

So my honest read: the assumption worth revising is narrow — "private APIs → automatic registry disqualification" was indeed too strong, and your experiment proves it. But that was never the decisive argument. The decisive arguments (fragility across updates, and the one-file/many-editors consistency problem that even Obsidian sidesteps by only ever mounting one nested editor at a time) stand exactly as before. If you ever want to revisit, the productive framing isn't "can we pass the scan" but "are we willing to own breakage on Obsidian updates, and do we have an answer to multi-editor sync that Obsidian itself avoided needing."

content.thinking:
So they're asking why we can't treat groups of lines as structural blocks at the DOM level rather than separate editor instances—essentially, what's the fundamental difference between syncing content blocks versus syncing individual lines? This gets at how CodeMirror 6 structures its document model and view layer, where lines are the atomic unit of the editor's internal representation, not arbitrary groupings we can easily promote to block-level constructs. The decoration system only offers three types — line attributes, widgets, and replacements — and none can wrap multiple lines in a shared parent. Tables work by using a replace decoration that tells CM6 to treat the entire multi-line range as an opaque widget, which means the plugin owns the DOM inside but loses all editor integration (cursor, selection, search) for that content, which is why Obsidian spawns a nested editor when you interact with it.

The user's idea is actually viable: render every block as a widget, then swap the focused block back to live lines on click. There's even a simpler variant where you keep one editor and only replace-decorate the inactive blocks, leaving the active one as normal editable lines — which is closer to how Live Preview already handles tables and embeds, revealing the source when the cursor enters rather than requiring a nested editor.

But there are real tradeoffs: you'd need to render each block's markdown preview yourself using the public MarkdownRenderer API, and you'd inherit all the widget problems we've identified — cursor navigation gets awkward with arrow keys skipping over atomic widgets, selection across blocks breaks, search highlighting doesn't work properly.

The honest take is that this proposal is essentially reimplementing what Live Preview already does — Obsidian already handles the "widget when unfocused, source when focused" pattern per block for embeds and tables. It's technically feasible with public APIs like Decoration.replace, but it's heavy in practice. Cross-block selection, find-in-note highlighting, spellcheck, vim mode, and other plugins' decorations all only work on live text, and performance suffers from re-rendering widgets on every edit elsewhere.

For tables specifically, you'd need a nested editor because the raw source form is unreadable — that's genuinely bad UX. But for outline nodes, the raw source *is* the desired editing form, so you get "one active editor" for free; it's just the outer editor itself. That's the key insight: the nested editor is only necessary when the source representation is unusable.

The current line-based approach works because everything stays as live lines and we only decorate on top — that preserves full native editing and all ecosystem features. The real tradeoff is DOM freedom versus how much native editing you keep, and blocks are just spans of lines that CodeMirror renders.

content.text:
Your clarification changes the picture meaningfully, so let me first concede the point, then answer the actual question — why lines can't just be grouped in the DOM — because that's the crux, and it has a precise technical answer.

## You're right: one-editor-at-a-time removes the sync objection

The doc's "N editors over overlapping ranges" objection was aimed at a persistent tree of live editors. Your design — static rendered tree, one live editor materialized under the focused node, torn down on blur — is exactly the table pattern, and it has the same property that makes tables tractable: at any moment there's one editor over one small range, so the multi-editor consistency problem never exists. That objection doesn't apply to your variant. What remains are different problems, and they live in your second question.

## Why you can't group lines in the DOM: the DOM isn't ours

The mental model to install is: **CM6's content DOM is not a document you build — it's a projection CM6 owns and continuously rewrites.** The contract is rigid: `.cm-content` contains a flat list of `.cm-line` elements, one per visible line, nothing in between. CM6 depends on that flatness for everything positional: mapping a mouse click to a text offset, placing the cursor rectangle, drawing selections, handling IME composition. On every update it diffs and patches this DOM itself — so even if you reached in and wrapped five lines in a `<div class="node">`, CM6's next reconciliation pass would move, recreate, or re-parent those lines and shred your wrapper. It's the same reason you can't manually edit React's rendered output: you'd be fighting the owner of the DOM.

The *sanctioned* ways to alter what's rendered are decorations, and there are only three shapes:

- **Line decorations** — add attributes/classes to a line element (what we do now: indentation, markers).
- **Widget decorations** — insert an inert element at a point.
- **Replace decorations** — hide a range entirely and optionally show one opaque widget in its place.

Notice what's missing: there is **no decoration type that wraps a range of lines in a shared parent element while keeping them live lines**. That's not an oversight; it would break the flat-DOM contract that position-mapping depends on. This is the whole technical gap, stated in one sentence.

## So "grouping" is possible — but it's a one-way door

You *can* group lines: that's a replace decoration over the block's multi-line range. But look at what it means. CM6 is told "this range is no longer text; here's one black box." The lines vanish from the DOM; inside the widget you can build any DOM you want — a table, a tree node card, anything. The price is that the black box is **inert to the editor**: no cursor can enter it, no selection can cross it, find-in-note can't highlight inside it, spellcheck and other plugins' decorations don't reach it, because to CM6 it's a single atomic character.

**That's precisely why the table needs the nested editor.** It's not an optimization — it's a forced move. Obsidian widget-replaced the table range to get free-form DOM, which made the text uneditable, so it must mount a fresh editor inside the widget the moment you click a cell, and privately sync it back. The nested editor is the *repair* for what replacement broke.

So the distinction you asked about — "blocks in editor views" vs "lines of text" — is really a binary each range must choose:

| | Live lines | Replaced widget |
|---|---|---|
| DOM shape | flat `.cm-line`s, un-groupable | anything you want |
| Editing, cursor, selection | native, free | dead — needs a grafted editor |
| Search/vim/other plugins | all work | all blind to it |

There is no middle state. Grouped-but-still-live doesn't exist in CM6.

## The interesting consequence for your proposal

Here's the part worth sitting with: your design is "everything is a rendered widget except the focused block." But notice — if the focused block is *not* replaced, it's just… ordinary live lines in the ordinary outer editor. **You don't need a nested editor at all.** Tables need one because a table's raw source (one giant pipe-delimited line) is unusable as an editing surface. An outline node's raw source is exactly the markdown you want the user typing into. So the "one active editor" in your scheme can simply be the outer editor showing the focused block as normal lines, while every *other* block is replace-decorated into a rendered card. No private API for that part — `Decoration.replace` and `MarkdownRenderer.render` are both public.

And here's the reveal: **that architecture already exists — it's Live Preview itself.** "Render blocks as widgets, un-render the one the cursor is in" is literally Obsidian's Live Preview algorithm; it just renders widgets as flat prose instead of a tree. Your proposal amounts to reimplementing Live Preview's render pipeline with tree-shaped DOM around the blocks.

That's not absurd — but the costs are now precisely nameable, and they're the widget-inertness costs, multiplied by "the whole document is widgets": arrow-key travel through atomic widgets is clunky (CM6 hops over them as single characters), selecting text across two blocks is impossible, note-wide search highlighting goes dark, every third-party editor plugin stops seeing your content, and you're re-rendering markdown previews for every block on every structural change. Our Experiment 1 already met the small version of this pain — the `.cm-embed-block` atoms (tables, callouts) that refused line decorations and needed the inline-style `ViewPlugin` workaround — and that was with only a *few* atoms per note.

So the honest summary: your instinct was architecturally sound (it's Live Preview's own trick), the N-editors objection genuinely doesn't apply to it, and the private-API footprint is smaller than the shelved variant — possibly near zero if the focused block stays in the outer editor. The real trade is different and cleaner to state: the current approach keeps 100% of native editing and decorates around it; yours buys free-form tree DOM by making every unfocused block invisible to the editor and everything built on it. That's a legitimate future experiment — "Live-Preview-style block widgetization" — but it competes against a decoration stack that, per experiments 1/2b/5a, is no longer struggling to convey the tree.

content.thinking:
So the distinction is that a flat CM6 model enforces tree invariants at the *data structure level* rather than through input interception—it validates the tree shape whenever the document changes, regardless of how that change happened (keyboard, mouse, paste, plugins, etc.), whereas keyboard tricks only catch the specific inputs you've enumerated and leave everything else unprotected. The transactionFilter intercepts every mutation—edits, selections, pastes, programmatic changes—at a single enforcement point, then validates whether each change preserves the tree structure; invalid mutations get vetoed, boundary-crossing ones get rewritten into structurally equivalent operations, and selection-only transactions can be normalized to node boundaries. Since every change must pass through this filter and the invariant starts valid, induction guarantees the buffer always parses to a well-formed tree regardless of how the edit originated.

The hard part is the rewrite logic itself—converting arbitrary text diffs into valid tree operations is the core engineering challenge, while keymaps become secondary affordances that produce already-valid transactions. Edge cases like IME composition, undo history interaction, incremental parsing performance, and Obsidian's own features (find-replace, spellcheck) all flow through the same filter, which is exactly the point—one place where text edits become tree operations or get rejected.

The key distinction: keymaps handle the ergonomics layer (making common operations easy), while the filter enforces validity (making invalid states unreachable). That's what separates this from obsidian-outliner's approach.

content.text:
You're not missing something — the feasibility research did answer this, but the answer lives in one dense phrase ("`transactionFilter` invariant layer", [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:144](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:144)) that deserves the unpacking you're asking for. Here it is in simple terms.

## The distinction: intercepting inputs vs. intercepting outcomes

What the manifest calls "keyboard tricks" is architecture, not effort: obsidian-outliner works by intercepting *inputs*. It rebinds Enter, Tab, Backspace, and handles each one specially. The fatal property of that approach is that its coverage is an enumerated list — anything not on the list walks straight past it: a mouse drag-selection that grabs half of one node and half of another, a paste, find-and-replace, a vim command, another plugin dispatching an edit, multi-cursor typing. That's exactly why "the structure is always one careless selection away from breaking" — the guards stand at *some* doors, and the document has many doors.

CM6 has a property that changes the game entirely: **every mutation of the document or selection, from every source, is expressed as a transaction, and all transactions pass through one funnel before applying.** Keystroke, mouse, paste, drag-drop, IME, search-replace, another plugin's programmatic edit — there is no side channel; nothing touches the buffer except through `dispatch`. A `transactionFilter` is a checkpoint installed in that funnel: it sees every proposed change *before it happens* and can pass it, rewrite it, or veto it.

The database analogy is the cleanest: keyboard tricks are validation code copy-pasted into every form of an app — miss one form and bad data gets in. A `transactionFilter` is a constraint in the database itself. It doesn't matter which app, which form, which script writes — the constraint holds, because there is no write path around it.

## How that enforces a tree over flat lines

The flat line buffer is just the *storage format*; the tree is a lens over it (our `parse()` already computes it). The invariant to enforce is: **the buffer always parses to a well-formed tree.** With a checkpoint on the only write path, you get that by induction — the document starts valid, and the filter guarantees every accepted transaction *preserves* validity, therefore it's valid forever. Concretely, for each incoming transaction the filter asks: what tree operation does this text change amount to?

- **It's already a valid tree op** (typing inside a node's text) → pass through untouched. The common case costs nothing.
- **It crosses a boundary but has a structural meaning** → *rewrite* it into the structural equivalent: a deletion spanning from mid-node-A to mid-node-B becomes "delete these whole nodes" or gets clamped to the boundary; a paste gets re-indented to a valid depth; a selection that crosses node boundaries gets escalated to whole-node selection (selection is part of the transaction too, so it's normalizable in the same place — pro-outliner ships a v1 of exactly this).
- **It has no valid structural meaning** (a change that would orphan children or corrupt a node header) → veto; the transaction simply never applies.

And here's the reframe that answers "aren't we going to end up with the same keyboard tricks": our keymaps (the Enter/Tab grammar we already have) stop being the enforcement and become mere *ergonomics* — convenient generators of already-valid transactions. The filter underneath is the actual invariant. obsidian-outliner has the first layer with nothing underneath; the whole thesis of this project is that second layer. Same keys, categorically different architecture.

## The honest caveats

The research flagged this as "High confidence, novel engineering" ([03:144](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:144)) and both words matter:

- **The rewrite logic is the hard, novel part.** Mapping an arbitrary text diff to "which tree operation is this?" is the real product — that's why the plan insists on the pure tree-ops + property-test discipline from the TUI prototype ([03:100](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:100)): the filter should delegate to a pure, exhaustively-tested `text diff → tree op | reject` function, not accumulate ad-hoc cases.
- **It must be fast**, since it runs on every keystroke — hence incremental parsing (the CM6 syntax tree, not a full re-parse).
- **Some genuinely fiddly corners exist**: IME composition mustn't be broken mid-stream by rewrites, and rewritten/vetoed transactions interact with undo history in ways that need care. These are known-solvable (filters are a designed-for CM6 feature, used in production), but they're where the engineering rigor goes.
- **Cursor motion** has its own primitive: `atomicRanges` makes structural markers un-enterable by the caret, so the cursor can't even *land* somewhere that would invite an invalid edit.

So the one-sentence answer to your question: the model being flat lines doesn't force input-level tricks, because CM6 offers a single choke point through which *all* edits flow — enforcement lives there, as a validator/rewriter of proposed changes against the parsed tree, and that is precisely the "invariant layer" the feasibility doc bet the architecture on. It's also worth noting this enforcement question is fully orthogonal to the decoration work we've been doing — decorations make the tree *visible*, the filter makes it *unbreakable*, and neither depends on the other's mechanism.

# outline-keyboard-grammar Specification

## Purpose
Defines the CM6 keyboard grammar that drives structural editing directly from the keyboard
in outline mode: which keys map to which structural operations and node-split behavior, how
the grammar scopes itself to outline-mode files, and the transaction/undo contract each
accepted keypress SHALL satisfy.

## Requirements
### Requirement: Grammar is scoped to outline mode
The keyboard grammar SHALL be registered as a CodeMirror extension via
`registerEditorExtension` and SHALL activate per keypress only when the editor's file has
outline mode enabled (resolved through the public `editorInfoField`). Outside outline
mode every grammar binding SHALL decline the key so editor behavior is byte-for-byte
stock Obsidian.

#### Scenario: Stock behavior off-mode
- **WHEN** Tab is pressed in a list in a note without outline mode
- **THEN** Obsidian's default indent behavior runs, unaffected by the plugin

#### Scenario: Toggle takes effect immediately
- **WHEN** outline mode is toggled while the note is open
- **THEN** the next keypress already follows the new mode, with no editor reload

### Requirement: Structural key bindings
In outline mode, high-precedence bindings SHALL map Tab → indent node, Shift+Tab →
outdent node, Alt+ArrowUp → move node up, Alt+ArrowDown → move node down, targeting the
node at the cursor line. Each accepted operation SHALL dispatch as one CM6 transaction
(annotated with a `userEvent`) forming a single undo step, with the selection placed at
the operation's cursor result. Rejections SHALL show the transient cue and change
